paragraphs.body: The Weight of Judgment: Self-Sabotage, Sobriety, and the Cost of Perception Value judgements... are loaded with a collection of rules and benchmarks that one has accumulated while seeking value and self-worth outside of oneself in interaction with others and the world. We are all measured, whether it be by our parents, our friends, our partners, or ourselves. These judgements don't only measure–they define. They seep into our skin day in and day out, regardless of how willing you may be to acknowledge them.

annotations.text: The Weight of Judgment: Self-Sabotage, Sobriety, and the Cost of Perception

paragraphs.body: Value judgements were quite prevalent in my relationship with my partner. I felt every inch of her gaze every time I drank too much and every lick of disapproval when I skipped class yet again. I was set on what she was quietly thinking to herself: You’re a mess. You’re screwing up your health and career. You’re failing endlessly. We end up rejecting our own experience as is and start wishing and wanting to distance ourselves from the inferior feeling or take action to change it for the better. Day by day. That’s what I told myself. Five days sober. Three months. Two hours. It was always a lingering thought in the back of my mind. I couldn't control myself, and it started controlling me. She tried to be patient with me, always encouraging my sobriety. I knew she was silently tallying all the times I had promised to "get better" and couldn't. All the elaborate lies I would make to cover my tracks when I did break my sobriety. The quiet exhaustion in her voice everytime she would realize what I was doing. It’s burned into my mind. The way she held me when I would come home drunk but didn’t meet my eyes in the morning. Her judgment—whether real or imagined—I can’t forget it, and so I drank to make it disappear. And then I drank more when I realized it never did.

One morning, I found a list on her phone: everything I had ever done while drunk and angry in case we ever broke up. I don’t know if it was meant as a safeguard, a record of pain, or proof of all the ways I had failed her. I wondered if my worst moments were all she saw in me, or if I had become those moments entirely.

annotations.text: One morning, I found a list on her phone: everything I had ever done while drunk and angry in case we ever broke up.

paragraphs.body: Value judgements... usually come with an emotional charge. In school, I was usually known for my potential and not my successes. My professors always told me I was smart and could go somewhere, but I would never turn in my assignments on time. ​ Truth be told, I never believed I could do more. I failed because I never tried. That was my pattern–waiting until things were too far gone so I could say to myself, See? It happened again. ​ Friends stopped inviting me out as much, knowing I would probably show up too plastered to be present and emotionally there. I sensed the shift in their initiation, saw how the invitations would slow down, and then eventually cease. ​ They never said it explicitly, but I could read their judgement in the space they left behind. Our life will then turn into a battleground of inner conflicts, filled with stress, perhaps temporary triumphs but back to depression or frustration and anger or self-doubt and helplessness. The night I fucked up too far to be forgiven, I was drunk. Except it wasn't the alcohol that made me do it, it was the guilt eating up at me, telling me that I had already ruined everything and had nothing left to lose. My relationship, my sobriety, my future. Nothing mattered anymore. ​ I remember waking up the next morning when she came into my apartment after she realized what I had done. When she left, she didn’t yell or ask me why. She just looked at me like I had confirmed what she always suspected. That I was incapable of being what she needed. That maybe I had always known it too. Judgement in and of itself is not harmful or unhealthy, in fact it is necessary for us to know and differentiate things. After she left me, the cycle continued. Drinking. Missing class. Surrounding myself with terrible influences. But this time, it felt emptier. I wasn’t fighting it anymore–I knew I could do better but I was waiting for someone to tell me what to do next. ​ But no one could. This was my life. And I had to decide whether I wanted to keep self-sabotaging everything good that has ever happened to me, or finally take accountability for my actions, for myself, for the real me–buried deep underneath the fear, the failure, and the judgements. ​ I am still recovering and learning more about myself everyday. I am still learning how to exist without defining myself by my worst moments. Value judgements–whether from others or myself–are only powerful as long as you give them power. Works Cited Hamid. "Helpful and Harmful Judgements: How to Know and What to Do." InnerChange Life Coaching, 4 May 2016.

annotations.text: I remember waking up the next morning when she came into my apartment after she realized what I had done. When she left, she didn’t yell or ask me why. She just looked at me like I had confirmed what she always suspected. That I was incapable of being what she needed. That maybe I had always known it too.
annotations.comment: Is this intentionally vague? (Or just too difficult to talk about...) This feels like it should, or at least could, be a moment of narrative. Which makes me wonder: what if you had more personal narrative woven throughout this piece?

This is an extremely capable and well-written summary, but this activity isn’t actually _about_ summary or report. Rather, we’re writing an essay with a central focusing idea, audience, and purpose based on your own agenda and developed with ideas, details, and examples from your own life. It’s much more personal, ultimately than an academic study (and, thus, hopefully much more fun!) Let’s explore and discuss what this topic means to you in our meeting (including audience and purpose). :)
